slides.title: Functions Without a Name
slides.body: A **lambda** is a tiny, one-line function you write right where you need it — no `def`, no name, no ceremony. Think of it as a sticky note instead of a formal memo.
slides.tip: Lambdas don't replace regular functions — they're for quick, throwaway logic.

slides.title: The Syntax in One Line
slides.body: The pattern is `lambda parameters: expression`. The expression is **automatically returned** — no `return` keyword needed.
slides.code:
# Regular function
def double(x):
    return x * 2

# Same thing as a lambda
lambda x: x * 2
slides.tip: Both do exactly the same thing — lambda is just more compact.

slides.title: Lambdas Shine with sorted()
slides.body: The most common use: pass a lambda as the `key=` argument to `sorted()`, `min()`, or `max()` to control **how** items are compared.
slides.code:
names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Di"]
result = sorted(names, key=lambda name: len(name))
print(result)
slides.tip: The lambda runs on each item and returns the value to sort by.

slides.title: map() and filter() Too
slides.body: `map()` transforms every item; `filter()` keeps only items where the function returns `True`. Lambdas slot in perfectly as the function argument.
slides.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
squared = list(map(lambda n: n ** 2, nums))
evens = list(filter(lambda n: n % 2 == 0, nums))
print(squared)
print(evens)
slides.tip: List comprehensions are often even cleaner for these cases.

slides.title: One Expression — That's the Limit
slides.body: A lambda can only hold a **single expression** — no loops, no `if` blocks, no `return`. When logic gets bigger, switch to a regular `def`.
slides.tip: If it doesn't fit comfortably on one line, it doesn't belong in a lambda.

slides.title: You've Got Lambda!
slides.body: Lambdas are quick, inline functions perfect for `key=` sorting and passing to `map`/`filter`. For anything more complex, `def` is your friend — **readability always wins**.
slides.tip: Rule of thumb: unnamed and inline → lambda. Named and reused → def.
